stem: Which of these are antagonist drugs?
explanation: Antagonists block receptor activation. Of these, atropine, propranolol and phentolamine are receptor antagonists; morphine and clonidine are agonists.

statements.explanation: Morphine is a μ-opioid receptor agonist, not an antagonist.
Atropine is a competitive muscarinic acetylcholine receptor antagonist.
Propranolol is a non-selective β-adrenoceptor antagonist.
Phentolamine is a non-selective α-adrenoceptor antagonist.
Clonidine is an α2-adrenoceptor agonist, not an antagonist.

explanation: The pneumotachograph (Fleisch type) measures flow from a pressure drop across a laminar-flow resistance; accuracy depends on gas composition and temperature.

statements.text: Measures pressure difference across a resistance
statements.explanation: The pneumotachograph measures the pressure drop across a fixed resistance to infer flow.

statements.text: Depends on laminar gas flow for accurate reading
statements.explanation: Accurate readings require laminar flow (Poiseuille relationship between flow and pressure drop).

statements.text: Requires recalibration if there is a change in gas composition
statements.explanation: Gas composition changes viscosity, requiring recalibration (notably with helium or N2O).

statements.text: Is not suitable for accurate breath-by-breath monitoring
statements.explanation: It has a very low dead-space and rapid response so it is suitable for breath-by-breath monitoring.

statements.text: Accuracy is unaffected by temperature change
statements.explanation: Temperature changes alter gas viscosity, so accuracy is affected by temperature.

explanation: EEG delta activity reflects depressed cortical function — from deep anaesthesia, severe hypotension or cerebral hypoxia.

statements.explanation: Delta waves (<4 Hz, high amplitude) typify deep anaesthesia and slow-wave sleep.
Marked hypotension causes cerebral hypoperfusion and EEG slowing into the delta range.
Cerebral hypoxia slows the EEG; delta activity appears as oxygenation falls.

stem: Drugs in pregnant women that cause respiratory depression in the fetus:
explanation: Lipid-soluble CNS depressants (opioids, benzodiazepines, high-dose local anaesthetics) cross the placenta and depress the fetus; muscle relaxants do not.

statements.explanation: Morphine crosses the placenta and depresses neonatal respiration.
IM pethidine is a classic cause of neonatal respiratory depression if given near delivery.

statements.text: Local anesthetic dose in adequate amounts
statements.explanation: Systemic absorption of large local-anaesthetic doses can depress the fetus.
Midazolam crosses the placenta and depresses neonatal respiration and tone.
Non-depolarising muscle relaxants are highly ionised and cross poorly; fetal respiratory depression is not expected.

stem: Question about vaporizers in low atmospheric pressure situations

explanation: Standard arterial/alveolar partial-pressure values at sea level: PaO2 ~100, PaCO2 ~40, PN2 ~573, PH2O 47, mixed-venous PO2 ~40 mmHg.

statements.explanation: Normal PaO2 in arterial blood is ~95–100 mmHg.
PN2 in alveolar/arterial gas is approximately 573–596 mmHg.
PH2O is ~47 mmHg at body temperature (fully saturated alveolar gas).
Mixed venous (right atrial) PO2 is ~40 mmHg; arterial PO2 in the right atrium would be the same as mixed venous.

explanation: Interscalene block targets the upper trunks at the cervical level — predictable phrenic paresis and frequent ulnar sparing; awake performance is standard.

statements.text: Acts at the level of the cords
statements.explanation: The interscalene block deposits anaesthetic at the level of the trunks, not the cords.

statements.text: The medial trunk is usually spared
statements.explanation: The lower (inferior) trunk (C8–T1) is often spared, producing ulnar-territory sparing.

statements.text: Can cause ipsilateral diaphragmatic paralysis in x% of cases
statements.explanation: Ipsilateral phrenic block and hemidiaphragmatic paresis occur in ~100% of interscalene blocks.
It is generally performed awake or lightly sedated so paraesthesiae can be reported.

stem: Anatomical references for median nerve block at the elbow:
explanation: At the elbow, the median nerve lies medial to the brachial artery just over the medial epicondyle — the artery and the epicondyle are the operative landmarks.

statements.explanation: The cephalic vein lies laterally at the elbow, not at the median nerve site.
The basilic vein is medial but not the immediate landmark for median-nerve block.
The medial epicondyle is the bony landmark; the nerve runs medial to brachial artery.
The biceps tendon is medial in the cubital fossa but the median nerve lies medial to the brachial artery, not the biceps head.
The brachial (humeral) artery is the key landmark — the median nerve lies just medial to it at the elbow.

stem: Expected nerve injuries from lithotomy position, Trendelenburg, and shoulder support after 2 hours of surgery:
explanation: Lithotomy/Trendelenburg/shoulder support typically injures the common peroneal (foot drop) and lateral femoral cutaneous (lateral-thigh numbness) nerves.

statements.explanation: Lithotomy + shoulder support classically injures the common peroneal nerve → foot drop.
Lateral cutaneous nerve of the thigh compression in lithotomy produces lateral-thigh numbness (meralgia).
Wrist drop is a radial-nerve injury, not typically caused by this position.
Urinary incontinence is not a recognised positional injury of this combination.

stem: Regarding pregnant women and epidural with local anesthetics:
explanation: Labour epidural smooths blood pressure (helpful in pre-eclampsia) and causes urinary retention; it does not relax the uterus or directly depress the neonate.

statements.text: Enhances the effect of vena cava compression
statements.explanation: Epidural causes sympathetic block and worsens supine vena-caval compression hypotension, but does not enhance the underlying compression itself; effect is on tolerance, not the mechanical compression.

statements.text: Facilitates blood pressure control in pre-eclampsia
statements.explanation: Epidural is recommended in pre-eclampsia: smooths blood-pressure control and reduces catecholamine surges.
Local-anaesthetic epidural blocks sensory/motor; it does not cause uterine relaxation.
Sacral block from epidural commonly causes urinary retention.
Plain local-anaesthetic epidural without opioid additives does not cause neonatal respiratory depression.

stem: Question about the peak of the R wave on the ECG and its correspondence in the cardiac cycle:
explanation: Electromechanical coupling: the R-wave marks the onset of ventricular systole — coincident with S1 — preceding aortic valve opening and the systolic pressure peak.

statements.explanation: R-wave peak coincides with the start of ventricular mechanical contraction (depolarisation triggers it).
Peak aortic pressure occurs later in systole, well after the R-wave.
Aortic valve opens after isovolumetric contraction, slightly after R-wave peak.
The first heart sound (S1: mitral/tricuspid closure) occurs at the QRS/R-wave peak.

stem: Graph with the y-axis as "receptor occupancy/effect" and the x-axis as "drug quantity." Three curves A, B, C. Curve A = B but shifted to the left on the x-axis. Curve C reaches only 50% of the y-axis (diagram would make it clearer)
explanation: Concentration–effect curves: full agonists reach 100% (parallel rightward shift implies a competitive antagonist or weaker agonist); a partial agonist plateaus below 100%.

statements.text: Drug A is a full agonist
statements.explanation: Curve A reaches 100% effect — it is a full agonist.

statements.text: Drug B is a full agonist
statements.explanation: B reaches 100% but at higher dose (rightward shift) — also a full agonist but less potent.

statements.text: Drug C occurs in the presence of a competitive antagonist
statements.explanation: A shift from A to A-position with maximum preserved is competitive antagonism; C reaching 50% is a partial agonist, not competitive antagonism.

statements.text: Shift from curve A to B in the presence of
statements.explanation: Shift from A to B (parallel, max preserved) is the signature of competitive antagonism, not the reverse direction.

statements.text: Fentanyl as drug A and sufentanil as drug B
statements.explanation: Sufentanil is more potent than fentanyl, so a leftward A vs rightward B fits sufentanil = A, fentanyl = B (matching the prompt’s orientation depending on labelling).

explanation: Central chemoreceptors in the medulla sense CSF H+ generated by diffused CO2; PO2 and plasma H+ act peripherally (carotid/aortic bodies).

statements.explanation: Central chemoreceptors respond to PCO2/pH in CSF, not PO2 (that is peripheral chemoreceptors).

statements.text: Respond to increased plasma hydrogen ion concentration
statements.explanation: Plasma H+ does not cross the blood–brain barrier readily; central response is to CSF H+.
Increased PCO2 diffuses into CSF where it forms H+ — strong central stimulus to ventilation.

statements.text: Respond to increased hydrogen ion concentration in the CSF
statements.explanation: Central chemoreceptors directly sense CSF H+ produced from dissolved CO2.
Lactate is not the central chemoreceptor stimulus.

explanation: Arterial baroreceptors fire more with stretch (hypertension) and less with hypotension; the reflex response to hypotension is decreased afferent firing.

statements.explanation: Baroreceptors are tonically active and their firing DECREASES with hypotension (unloading), causing reflex sympathetic activation.

stem: The afferent pathway of the carotid sinus is (repeated):
explanation: Carotid sinus baroreceptor afferents travel via the glossopharyngeal nerve (CN IX, Hering’s nerve) to the nucleus tractus solitarius.

statements.explanation: The glossopharyngeal nerve (CN IX) carries the carotid sinus baroreceptor afferents via the sinus nerve (of Hering).
Vagus and other cranial nerves are not the primary afferent — the rest are indeed false.

explanation: ACh is the neurotransmitter at all autonomic preganglionic synapses, parasympathetic postganglionic synapses, sympathetic-cholinergic sweat-gland fibres, and the motor end-plate.

statements.explanation: Sweat glands receive sympathetic cholinergic innervation — ACh is the transmitter.
Adrenal medulla is innervated by preganglionic sympathetic fibres using ACh.
All autonomic preganglionic transmission, including sympathetic ganglia, is cholinergic.
Parasympathetic ganglia (including the otic ganglion to parotid) use ACh.
Motor end-plate uses ACh acting on nicotinic receptors.

explanation: Amide local anaesthetics (lidocaine, ropivacaine, bupivacaine) are hepatically metabolised; esters (procaine) are hydrolysed by plasma cholinesterase; prilocaine has dual hepatic/pulmonary metabolism.

statements.explanation: Prilocaine is metabolised by both liver and lung (and to o-toluidine causing methaemoglobinaemia).
Lidocaine is metabolised primarily by hepatic CYP enzymes.
Ropivacaine is hepatically metabolised by CYP1A2 and CYP3A4.
Procaine is an ester, hydrolysed by plasma cholinesterase (not primarily hepatic).
Bupivacaine is metabolised in the liver (CYP3A4).

explanation: Most filtered sodium is reclaimed in proximal tubule + loop of Henle; the distal nephron handles fine adjustments. Creatinine FE is normally low.

statements.explanation: Fractional excretion of creatinine is typically <10%, but can rise to 10% or more in conditions of high tubular secretion / glomerular hyperfiltration.

statements.text: Sodium is mostly reabsorbed in the distal tubule
statements.explanation: Sodium is mostly reabsorbed in the proximal tubule (~65%) and loop of Henle (~25%); only ~5% reaches the distal tubule.

explanation: Mannitol is a classic agent for emergency IOP lowering via osmotic withdrawal of vitreous fluid.

statements.explanation: IV mannitol osmotically reduces vitreous volume, lowering intra-ocular pressure rapidly.

explanation: ACE inhibitors and aldosterone antagonists raise K+; β2-agonists, loop and thiazide diuretics typically lower it.

statements.explanation: Atenolol can cause modest hyperkalaemia via β2-blockade but is not a typical cause.
ACE inhibitors block aldosterone secretion and impair K+ excretion → hyperkalaemia.
Thiazides cause HYPOkalaemia, not hyperkalaemia.
Bumetanide (loop diuretic) causes HYPOkalaemia.
β2-agonists shift K+ INTO cells, producing HYPOkalaemia.

explanation: Physiological right-to-left shunt is contributed by the Thebesian and bronchial veins; the ductus arteriosus closes after birth.

statements.explanation: Thebesian veins drain directly into the left chambers — a small physiological right-to-left shunt.
Bronchial veins drain into pulmonary veins (left atrium), adding to the physiological shunt.
The ductus arteriosus is a left-to-right shunt postnatally (or closed); not a right-to-left shunt source.

stem: A previously healthy man who, after trauma, underwent resection of 50% of the liver. In the postoperative period, it is expected that he will develop:
explanation: After major hepatectomy, the liver’s synthetic functions transiently fail — leading to low albumin, low total protein and low fibrinogen.

statements.explanation: Hepatic synthetic dysfunction after major resection commonly causes hypoalbuminaemia.
Overall plasma protein synthesis is reduced postoperatively.
Fibrinogen is hepatically synthesised; its plasma level falls after major hepatectomy.

explanation: Albumin: ~66.5 kDa, half-life ~20 days, ~55–60% of plasma protein, falls in acute-phase response (negative acute-phase protein).

statements.explanation: Albumin half-life is ~20 days, not 3 days.
Albumin molecular weight is ~66,500 Da, not 40,000.
Albumin is a NEGATIVE acute-phase protein — its level falls with inflammation.
Albumin accounts for ~55–60% of plasma proteins.

explanation: Insulin: half-life ~5–8 min; ~60% renal clearance, ~30% hepatic. Renal failure prolongs insulin action.

statements.explanation: Around 60% (not 10%) of insulin is degraded by the kidney.
Plasma half-life of endogenous insulin is ~5–8 minutes; 1.5 hours is far too long.

stem: The apparent volume of distribution at steady state of a drug administered intravenously (repeated):
explanation: Vd = dose / plasma concentration; it is a drug-specific constant that can far exceed total body water for lipophilic, tissue-binding drugs.

statements.explanation: Vd is a drug-specific pharmacokinetic constant.

statements.text: Is closely related to the size of anatomical fluid compartments
statements.explanation: It reflects how widely a drug distributes relative to body fluid compartments.

statements.text: Cannot exceed the total body volume
statements.explanation: Highly tissue-bound drugs (digoxin, amiodarone) have Vd >> total body volume.

statements.text: Is inversely related to plasma drug concentration
statements.explanation: Vd is inversely related to plasma concentration at a given dose (Vd = dose / [drug]plasma).
Higher lipid solubility increases tissue uptake and therefore raises Vd.

explanation: Vecuronium: amino-steroid, hepatic metabolism, very stable haemodynamics, fully reversible with sugammadex; phenytoin alters its kinetics.

statements.text: Is less cardiovascularly stable than atracurium
statements.explanation: Vecuronium is highly cardiovascularly stable (no histamine release); atracurium can release histamine.

statements.text: Is absolutely contraindicated in hepatic failure
statements.explanation: Vecuronium can be used cautiously in hepatic failure with prolonged duration; not absolute contraindication.
Sugammadex reversibly encapsulates vecuronium and reverses its block.
Phenytoin chronically induces metabolism and shortens block, but acutely potentiates it.
Vecuronium is metabolised by the liver, not pseudocholinesterase (that is mivacurium).

stem: Drugs that cross the blood-brain barrier:
explanation: Lipid-soluble small molecules cross the BBB (scopolamine); polar catecholamines like dopamine do not.

statements.explanation: Scopolamine is highly lipid-soluble and crosses the BBB (causing anti-emesis and sedation).
Dopamine is polar and does NOT cross the BBB; this is why Parkinson’s is treated with levodopa.

explanation: Dabigatran has antibody reversal (idarucizumab); factor Xa inhibitors are reversed by andexanet alfa; both need ~48 h cessation pre high-bleeding-risk surgery.

statements.explanation: No specific antibody reverses apixaban; andexanet alfa is a decoy factor Xa, not an antibody.
Idarucizumab is a monoclonal antibody fragment that reverses dabigatran.

statements.text: High bleeding risk surgery: discontinue apixaban and rivaroxaban 2 days before
statements.explanation: Apixaban and rivaroxaban are usually stopped 2 days before high bleeding-risk surgery (renal function dependent).

explanation: Plasma osmolarity ≈ 2×[Na] + [glucose] + [urea]; depends on particle number not charge; albumin contributes oncotic pressure, not osmolality.

statements.text: A measure of the number of particles in solution
statements.explanation: Osmolarity reflects the number of osmotically active particles per litre of solution.

statements.text: Commonly determined by the temperature at which a solution freezes
statements.explanation: Freezing-point depression osmometry is the standard laboratory method.

statements.text: Usually expressed in milliosmoles per liter
statements.explanation: It is reported in milliosmoles per litre (mOsm/L) or per kilogram (mOsm/kg, osmolality).

statements.text: Proportional to the valency of the particles in solution
statements.explanation: Osmolarity depends on particle number, not their valency.

statements.text: Dependent upon the serum albumin concentration
statements.explanation: Calculated osmolarity uses Na, glucose, urea — albumin is negligible (oncotic, not osmotic).

stem: Concerning the cardiac cycle (very similar, changes in the last two options):
explanation: Cardiac cycle: isovolumetric contraction has no volume change; atrial kick is ~25% of LVEDV; aortic valve opens once LV pressure exceeds aortic.

statements.text: Mean aortic pressure peaks at the end of systole
statements.explanation: Aortic pressure peaks mid-systole, not at end systole.

statements.text: At a heart rate of 70 beats per minute, atrial contraction contributes 60% of ventricular filling
statements.explanation: At HR 70/min the atrial kick contributes ~20–30% of filling, not 60%.

statements.text: The mitral and pulmonary valves open and close at the same time
statements.explanation: Mitral closes before aortic opens; pulmonary and tricuspid valves are on opposite circulations.

statements.text: Ventricular volume does not change during isometric contraction
statements.explanation: Isovolumetric (isometric) contraction — by definition no volume change with all valves closed.

statements.text: The aortic valve opens when ventricular contraction begins
statements.explanation: The aortic valve only opens when LV pressure EXCEEDS aortic — after isovolumetric contraction begins.

stem: The proportion (%) of inhaled anesthetic agents biotransformed is (repeated):
explanation: Volatile agent biotransformation order: sevoflurane (~5%) > halothane > isoflurane > desflurane (<0.1%); xenon and N2O are essentially inert.

statements.explanation: Isoflurane biotransformation ~0.2%; "1.7%" cited values reflect older data, accepted ~true range.
Desflurane: lowest metabolism (<0.1%) of volatile agents.
Sevoflurane: ~3–5% biotransformed to compound A and hexafluoroisopropanol.
Nitrous oxide: essentially not metabolised (<0.004%).
Xenon is inert and undergoes no biotransformation; 10% is wrong.

explanation: Severe exercise: VO2 and minute ventilation surge, PVR falls with recruitment, mixed-venous O2 sat drops sharply, and core temperature rises above 40 °C.

statements.text: Oxygen saturation of mixed venous blood remains above 70%
statements.explanation: Mixed venous saturation FALLS to ~20–30% in severe exercise (high tissue O2 extraction).

statements.text: Minute volume of ventilation may reach 130 liters
statements.explanation: Trained athletes can reach minute ventilation 120–150 L/min.
Pulmonary vascular resistance falls during exercise due to capillary recruitment and distension.
Cardiac output reaches 25 L/min in athletes — 50 L/min is too high.
Core temperature can exceed 40 °C during prolonged severe exercise.

explanation: Trans-thoracic impedance is reduced by good electrode contact (gel) and larger paddle surface area; repeated shocks have a small effect on impedance.

statements.explanation: Conducting gel reduces skin–paddle impedance and lowers resistance.
Repeated shocks reduce thoracic impedance only marginally.
Number of shocks alone is not a recognised determinant of resistance to current.
Larger paddle surface area lowers current density and impedance.

explanation: Surfactant (DPPC, type II pneumocytes) reduces alveolar surface tension, raises compliance, prevents alveolar collapse, and is depleted by loss of perfusion.

statements.text: Increases the surface tension of alveolar fluid
statements.explanation: Surfactant REDUCES surface tension (it is the key alveolar-stability function).
Lower surface tension increases pulmonary compliance.
Surfactant is a phospholipid–protein complex (mostly DPPC), not a mucopolysaccharide.

statements.text: Allows alveoli of different sizes to have the same surface tension
statements.explanation: Surfactant equalises pressures across alveoli of different sizes by lowering surface tension more in smaller alveoli.

statements.text: After prolonged interruption of pulmonary blood flow, its quantity decreases
statements.explanation: Loss of pulmonary perfusion (atelectasis) decreases surfactant production by type II pneumocytes.

explanation: Z-drugs bind the GABA_A benzodiazepine site, are reversed by flumazenil, suppress REM and produce tolerance/dependence.

statements.text: Act at the same site as benzodiazepines - GABA A
statements.explanation: Z-drugs act at the benzodiazepine-binding site on GABA_A receptors.
They REDUCE REM sleep (similar to benzodiazepines).
Their action is reversed by flumazenil (same site).
Zopiclone half-life ~5 h vs zolpidem ~2 h — within the class, zopiclone is longer; eszopiclone/zolpidem are short. Statement is conventionally accepted in EDAIC context.
Z-drugs cause dependence and tolerance similar to benzodiazepines.

explanation: Antifungals: echinocandin for invasive Candida; voriconazole/amphotericin for Aspergillus; C. krusei is fluconazole-resistant; azoles inhibit CYP3A4.

statements.text: Treatment of Candida krusei is sensitive to fluconazole
statements.explanation: Candida krusei is intrinsically RESISTANT to fluconazole.
Invasive candidiasis (especially in critical-care) is first-line treated with an echinocandin.
Aspergillosis is treated with voriconazole or amphotericin B (liposomal preferred).

statements.text: Amphotericin B - hepatic, renal toxicity, electrolyte imbalances
statements.explanation: Amphotericin B causes hepatic and renal toxicity plus K/Mg wasting.
Azoles (CYP3A4 inhibition) interfere with many co-administered drugs.

explanation: TBW ~60% adult, ~80% neonate; ICF:ECF ≈ 2:1; vomiting → hypokalaemic hypochloraemic alkalosis with hyponatraemia; water intoxication → hyponatraemia.

statements.text: The ratio of intracellular fluid to extracellular fluid (ICF:ECF) is 1:2
statements.explanation: ICF:ECF ratio is approximately 2:1, not 1:2 — TBW ~60% is split into ~40% ICF, ~20% ECF.

statements.text: TBW may be as high as 80% in the neonate
statements.explanation: Neonatal TBW is ~75–80% of body weight.

statements.text: When a person is dehydrated, plasma sodium may still be within the normal range
statements.explanation: In isotonic dehydration, plasma sodium remains within normal range.

statements.text: With severe vomiting, hypernatremia and hypokalemia will result
statements.explanation: Severe vomiting causes HYPOnatraemia + hypokalaemia + hypochloraemic metabolic alkalosis.

statements.text: In water intoxication, the plasma sodium level is always lower than normal
statements.explanation: Water intoxication dilutes plasma sodium — hyponatraemia is the defining feature.

stem: Among the recommendations for preoperative fasting
explanation: ESA fasting: 6 h solids, 4 h breast milk, 2 h clear fluids; delayed-emptying patients need individualised, prolonged fasting.

statements.text: In newborns, breast milk is allowed up to 4 hours before surgery
statements.explanation: Breast milk is considered like a clear liquid for some guidelines — 4 h fast in neonates is standard.

statements.text: A fasting period of 2 hours for clear liquids in all patients
statements.explanation: ESA guidelines: 2 h fast for clear liquids in all patients.

statements.text: Surgery should be postponed in patients who have chewed gum immediately before anesthesia induction
statements.explanation: Chewing gum or sweets immediately before induction does not mandate postponement under modern guidance.

statements.text: The volume of liquid ingested is more important than the type of liquid ingested
statements.explanation: Volume of clear liquid matters less than the gastric-emptying half-time of the fluid type, but volume guides intake limits; this is debatable but generally type matters more.

statements.text: Patients with delayed gastric emptying can follow the same guidelines as healthy adults
statements.explanation: Patients with delayed gastric emptying need longer fasts and aspiration precautions.

stem: In the anesthetic management of tetralogy of Fallot
explanation: ToF anaesthesia: maintain SVR, avoid tachycardia, treat spells immediately. Reducing SVR or PVR/SVR worsens cyanotic shunting.

statements.explanation: AVOID a fall in SVR — it worsens right-to-left shunt and hypercyanotic spells.

statements.text: Ventilatory measures to increase pulmonary vascular resistance (PVR)
statements.explanation: Increase PVR is wrong as a goal — you want to keep PVR low; but ventilatory manoeuvres can change PVR. Statement as written (intentionally raising PVR) is incorrect; treat as F is also defensible. Conventional ATI answer for ToF is to maintain low PVR / high SVR.
Tachycardia reduces ventricular filling and exacerbates dynamic outflow obstruction — avoid.

statements.text: Avoid a reduction in the pulmonary-to-systemic vascular resistance ratio (PVR/SVR)
statements.explanation: A reduction in PVR/SVR worsens right-to-left shunt — avoid.
Hypercyanotic spells must be treated immediately (squat, fluids, phenylephrine, β-blocker).

explanation: PRIS: high-dose long-duration propofol → mitochondrial dysfunction with lactic acidosis, rhabdomyolysis and cardiac failure; lipid load is significant.

statements.explanation: PRIS is caused by impaired mitochondrial fatty-acid oxidation.
>4 mg/kg/h for >24–48 h is the classic risk threshold.
Symptoms are metabolic ACIDOSIS (not alkalosis), rhabdomyolysis, cardiac failure.

statements.text: At high doses, it should be included in caloric calculations
statements.explanation: Propofol is delivered in lipid emulsion; high-dose infusions add significant calories.
PRIS has unpredictable individual susceptibility (idiosyncratic component).

explanation: Neonates need ~2× adult per-kg suxamethonium dose mainly because of larger extracellular volume of distribution; their plasma cholinesterase is lower, not higher.

statements.explanation: Increased Vd in neonates means more drug needed per kg to reach effective tissue concentration.
Neonatal pseudocholinesterase activity is LOWER than adult, not higher; metabolism is slower.
Renal excretion of suxamethonium is negligible — metabolism is by plasma cholinesterase.
Number of neuromuscular junctions does not vary by age in a way that explains dose changes.
Pseudocholinesterase activity is REDUCED in neonates, not increased.

explanation: Leuko-depleted RBCs reduce immunomodulation, HLA alloimmunisation, febrile non-haemolytic reactions and CMV transmission; they do not prevent prion transmission.

statements.explanation: Leuko-reduction reduces transfusion-related immunomodulation (TRIM).
Removes leukocytes that carry HLA antigens, reducing anti-HLA alloimmunisation.

statements.text: Prevents the risk of non-hemolytic febrile transfusion reactions
statements.explanation: Reduces but does not fully prevent non-haemolytic febrile reactions.
Leuko-reduction does not affect prion transmission (prions associate with plasma, not leukocytes).
CMV resides in leukocytes; depletion substantially lowers CMV transmission risk.

stem: Patient with multiple allergic reactions, how to document?
explanation: Documenting drug allergy: skin testing + written record are the standard; specific IgE and histamine release tests are useful adjuncts.

statements.explanation: Skin prick / intradermal testing is the gold standard for IgE-mediated allergy documentation.
Written documentation in the allergy passport/record is essential.
Basophil activation test exists but is research-tier, not routine.
Histamine release leukocyte test is used in specialist labs.
Specific IgE measurement (RAST) supplements skin testing.

explanation: Recurrent laryngeal nerve injury: thyroid/parathyroid surgery, neck dissections (carotid endarterectomy), left thoracic surgery (PDA, pneumonectomy), and ETT cuff pressure.

statements.explanation: Over-inflated cuffs can compress the recurrent laryngeal nerve at the cricoid.
Carotid-subclavian bypass is not a recognised cause.
Carotid endarterectomy may injure the recurrent laryngeal nerve in the neck dissection.
PDA ligation (left-sided thoracic surgery) risks left recurrent laryngeal injury.
Pneumonectomy / left thoracic surgery can damage the left recurrent laryngeal nerve.

explanation: Myotonic dystrophy: avoid suxamethonium and anticholinesterase reversal (both trigger myotonia); non-depolarisers do not abolish the myotonic response.

statements.explanation: Spinal anaesthesia is acceptable in myotonic dystrophy with careful technique.
Suxamethonium can precipitate a myotonic contracture — contraindicated.

statements.text: Reversal of non-depolarizing muscle relaxants leads to contractures
statements.explanation: Reversal with anticholinesterases can paradoxically trigger myotonic contractures.

statements.text: Non-depolarizing muscle relaxants do not attenuate myotonia
statements.explanation: Non-depolarising relaxants do not relieve the myotonic contraction (which is intrinsic to muscle membrane).

stem: Pregnant woman with heart failure and mitral stenosis

explanation: Post-CPB bleeding is multifactorial: dilutional/consumptive thrombocytopaenia + acquired vWF deficiency + low fibrinogen + heparin effect.

statements.explanation: CPB exposes blood to artificial surfaces and causes loss of large vWF multimers (acquired vWF deficiency).
Fibrinogen is consumed and diluted during CPB — common cause of microvascular bleeding.
Platelet count and function fall during CPB — major contributor to bleeding.

explanation: Steroids are the classical iatrogenic precipitant of DKA via hyperglycaemia and counter-regulatory effects.

statements.explanation: ACE inhibitors do not typically precipitate DKA.
Corticosteroids cause hyperglycaemia and can precipitate DKA in diabetics.
Loop diuretics may worsen dehydration but are not classical DKA precipitants.
Thiazides may worsen glucose control but are not classical DKA precipitants.
NSAIDs do not precipitate DKA.

explanation: Hypothyroidism: bradycardia, low CO, low Na, slow metabolism, increased sensitivity to anaesthetics/relaxants, low-voltage ECG.

statements.explanation: Hypothyroid patients are MORE sensitive to hypnotics, not resistant.
Hyponatraemia is common in hypothyroidism due to impaired free-water excretion.
Hypothyroidism causes bradycardia, low cardiac output and poor ventricular performance.

statements.text: Increased sensitivity to muscle relaxants (unspecified if depolarizing or non-depolarizing)
statements.explanation: Increased sensitivity to muscle relaxants (especially non-depolarisers).
Hypothyroidism ECG shows LOW voltage, T-wave flattening — not prominent P waves.

stem: COPD post-cholecystectomy, with altered consciousness and acidosis:
explanation: COPD with altered LOC and acidosis needs intubation; bicarbonate corrects the symptom not the cause. CPAP is useful pre-intubation; titrate, do not flood with O2.

statements.explanation: High FiO2 is unhelpful and risks CO2 narcosis in CO2-retainers — start with controlled O2.
NMB monitoring is mandatory perioperatively in compromised respiratory patients.
Intubation is indicated for altered consciousness with acidosis and impending respiratory failure.
CPAP/NIV may delay or avoid intubation in cooperative COPD patients.
Bicarbonate is not first-line — fix ventilation and oxygenation.

explanation: LVEDV (preload) falls with vasodilation (epidural, ACE-i, anaphylaxis) and with impaired right or left ventricular filling/output.

statements.explanation: AMI reduces LV systolic + diastolic function → reduced LVEDV in cardiogenic shock.
PE causes right heart failure with septal shift, reducing LV preload and LVEDV.
Epidural sympathectomy reduces venous return and LV preload.
ACE inhibitors reduce preload (venodilation) and afterload.
Anaphylaxis causes massive vasodilation and capillary leak — falling preload and LVEDV.

explanation: AKI: pre-renal urine is concentrated with low FeNa <1% and high urine/plasma osmolality; ATN urine is isosthenuric with FeNa >2%.

statements.explanation: In oliguria, urinary creatinine remains higher than plasma (concentrated urine).

statements.text: Urinary urea/creatinine ratio above 20 is pre-renal
statements.explanation: Urine urea/creatinine >20 (or BUN/Cr >20:1) suggests pre-renal aetiology.

statements.text: Acute tubular necrosis with low urine sodium fractional excretion
statements.explanation: ATN has HIGH (not low) fractional excretion of sodium (>2%) because tubules cannot concentrate.

statements.text: Acute tubular necrosis with urine density between 1.003 and 1.005
statements.explanation: ATN urine specific gravity is fixed around isosthenuric ~1.010, not 1.003–1.005.
ATN urine osmolality is iso-osmotic (~300), not concentrated 100 reflects diabetes insipidus.

explanation: Anatomy for double-lumen tube placement: right main bronchus is short and ~14 mm wide; tracheal pig-bronchus variant in ~3%; tracheal length ~12 cm.

statements.text: Bronchial cuff should not be visible when in the tracheal tube
statements.explanation: Bronchial cuff should NOT be visible above the carina when correctly placed in the bronchus.

statements.text: 3% of right upper lobe originates from the trachea/carina
statements.explanation: Right upper-lobe bronchus origin may be very high — ~3% of patients have it from carina/trachea (pig bronchus).

statements.text: Right bronchus diameter is 4.3 cm to 4.9 mm
statements.explanation: Right main bronchus diameter ~12–16 mm, not 4.3 cm; the figure is wrong.
Adult trachea ~10–12 cm length — approximately correct.

stem: Causes of cardiovascular collapse in a polytrauma patient:
explanation: In polytrauma collapse think haemorrhage, neurogenic shock (high cord injury), fat embolism, tension pneumothorax — not brain death or cerebral oedema acutely.

statements.explanation: Brain death itself does not cause acute cardiovascular collapse — the haemodynamic instability follows the autonomic storm and is later.
Fat embolism can cause shock from RV obstruction and inflammation.
Epidural hematoma with uncontrolled bleeding produces haemorrhagic shock and intracranial mass effect.

statements.text: Spinal cord injury with block up to T4
statements.explanation: High spinal/cord injury at T4 produces neurogenic shock with sympathetic loss.
Cerebral oedema causes raised ICP and Cushing’s response, but not primary cardiovascular collapse.

explanation: Digoxin toxicity: lidocaine for VT, atropine/pacing for bradyarrhythmias, Fab fragments for severe toxicity; avoid IV calcium and verapamil in classic teaching.

statements.text: Supraventricular tachycardia with rapid ventricular response
statements.explanation: Digoxin toxicity classically produces atrial tachyarrhythmias with AV block — SVT with rapid response can occur but typically with block.

Calcium should be AVOIDED in classic teaching for digoxin-induced hyperK (risk of stone heart). Statement “treated with calcium” is FALSE — but some recent literature challenges this. Conservative ATI answer marks it false/cautious.
Verapamil can worsen AV block and toxicity — generally avoided.
Lidocaine is used for digoxin-induced ventricular arrhythmias.

statements.text: Use of anti-digoxin antibody to treat hyperkalemia
statements.explanation: Digoxin-specific Fab fragments reverse toxicity, including life-threatening hyperkalaemia.

explanation: Diabetic coma (DKA): hyperglycaemia → glycosuria → osmotic diuresis → volume depletion; metabolic acidosis with total-body K+ depletion despite normal/high serum K+.

statements.explanation: Glycosuria reflects hyperglycaemia exceeding renal threshold.
Ketoacidosis is the metabolic acidosis of diabetic coma (high anion gap).
Intracellular K+ is LOW (extracellular K+ may be high initially but total body K+ depleted).
pH is DECREASED (acidosis), not increased.
Severe osmotic diuresis causes plasma volume depletion.

explanation: Cardiogenic shock from MI: maintain MAP (norepinephrine), oxygenate, consider IABP/LVAD; nitroglycerin is contraindicated in hypotension.

statements.explanation: IABP improves coronary perfusion and reduces afterload in cardiogenic shock.
LVAD is mechanical circulatory support for refractory cardiogenic shock.
Norepinephrine restores MAP and coronary perfusion in cardiogenic shock with hypotension.
Nitroglycerin reduces afterload but causes hypotension — risky if already hypotensive.
Supplemental O2 to SatO2 >94% is standard ACS care.

stem: Gas embolism in posterior fossa surgery:
explanation: VAE management: stop entrainment (flood/cover, lower head, jugular compression), aspirate via central catheter, support haemodynamics, FiO2 1.0.

statements.explanation: Saline-soaked compresses on the surgical field prevent further air entrainment.

statements.text: Aspiration through a multi-orifice catheter in the right atrium
statements.explanation: A multi-orifice central catheter in the right atrium allows air aspiration — key treatment.
Turn patient LEFT lateral DECUBITUS (Durant manoeuvre) to keep air in RV apex — statement “turn to the left side” is correct in some translations; ambiguous. Generally TRUE.
Compressing jugular veins increases CVP and stops further entrainment.
Mannitol does not treat venous air embolism.

explanation: Ophthalmic anaesthesia: control of IOP and systemic BP is crucial; acetazolamide → hypokalaemia; 10% phenylephrine drops are hazardous.

statements.explanation: Acetazolamide is a carbonic anhydrase inhibitor — causes HYPOkalaemia, not hyper.
Mannitol half-life ~5–6 h is approximately correct.

statements.text: Grade 3 hypertension, treat with antihypertensives and postpone surgery for 2 weeks
statements.explanation: Severe (grade 3) hypertension justifies postponing elective ophthalmic surgery.

statements.text: 10% phenylephrine drops cause severe hypertensive crises
statements.explanation: 10% phenylephrine drops are systemically absorbed and can cause hypertensive crises.

statements.text: Patient with enlarged eye, control heart rate and blood pressure for suprachoroidal surgery
statements.explanation: Open globe / suprachoroidal cases need controlled HR/BP to avoid sympathetic surges.

stem: Iron deficiency anemia and major surgery:
explanation: PBM principle: diagnose and treat anaemia preoperatively with iron first-line; EPO is reserved for selected patients.

statements.explanation: In non-oncologic elective surgery, postpone to optimise haemoglobin if anaemic.
Patient Blood Management is the framework of choice.

statements.text: Patients with iron deficiency anemia, first-line treatment is EPO
statements.explanation: First-line for IDA is iron therapy (oral/IV), not EPO; EPO is adjunct in selected cases.

explanation: Prolonged vomiting → hypokalaemic, hypochloraemic metabolic alkalosis with dehydration, often hyponatraemia and chronic nutritional deficits.

statements.explanation: Chronic vomiting depletes potassium.
Loss of HCl in vomit causes hypochloraemic alkalosis.
B12 absorption requires intrinsic factor; chronic vomiting can compromise dietary intake/absorption.
Uraemia is not characteristic of chronic vomiting (unless severely dehydrated → pre-renal).
Chronic vomiting may produce hyponatraemia from fluid loss plus ADH-mediated retention.

stem: Risk of hypoxemia when closing volume exceeds one of these:
explanation: Hypoxaemia occurs when closing volume exceeds FRC — small airways close during tidal breathing, producing shunt.

statements.explanation: Closing volume > residual volume occurs in normal ageing without hypoxaemia.
When closing volume EXCEEDS FRC, small airways close during tidal breathing → hypoxaemia.
IRV is not relevant to closing-volume hypoxaemia.
ERV is not the relevant comparator.
Vital capacity is not the relevant comparator.

explanation: Aspiration management: support respiration, bronchoscopy for solids, antibiotics only if infection develops, CT to assess lung; H2 blockers reduce acidity preventively.

statements.explanation: H2 blockers reduce gastric acidity and severity of chemical pneumonitis if aspiration occurs.
Antibiotics are reserved for established aspiration pneumonia, not prophylaxis.
Therapeutic bronchoscopy to remove solid aspirate is indicated.

statements.text: Measurement of O2 content in mixed venous blood
statements.explanation: Mixed venous O2 measurement is not specific management for aspiration.
CT can confirm parenchymal involvement and complications.

explanation: Post-tonsillectomy bleeding: lateral/head-down position, awake extubation, prepare blood; assume full stomach (swallowed blood).

statements.explanation: Post-tonsillectomy bleeding requires HEAD-DOWN/lateral or sitting position, not supine.
Premedication for anxiety/secretions is appropriate.
Extubation awake protects against aspiration of blood.
Post-tonsillectomy haemorrhage rarely causes severe shock; typically swallowed blood.

statements.text: Do not induce until blood is prepared
statements.explanation: Cross-matched blood should be available before induction in active bleeders.

stem: Diseases related to acetylcholine release at the neuromuscular junction:
explanation: NMJ diseases: myasthenia (postsynaptic AChR antibodies), Lambert-Eaton (presynaptic VGCC), botulism (presynaptic ACh release blocked), tetanus (CNS, indirectly NMJ).

statements.explanation: Myasthenia gravis: autoantibodies against postsynaptic ACh receptors.
Tetanus toxin blocks inhibitory NT release — peripheral effect involves enhanced ACh action and motor end plate excitability.
Myotonic dystrophy is a muscle membrane disorder, not primarily ACh release.

explanation: Cor pulmonale = right heart failure secondary to lung pathology. Chronic hypoxic vasoconstriction (COPD, emphysema, pneumoconioses) is the typical cause.

statements.explanation: Silicosis causes restrictive lung disease with pulmonary hypertension and cor pulmonale.
Berylliosis (chronic) causes granulomatous restriction and cor pulmonale.
Emphysema is a major cause of chronic cor pulmonale.
COPD is the most common cause of cor pulmonale.
Aortic stenosis causes LEFT-sided heart failure, not cor pulmonale.

stem: Factors contributing to hypotension following release of the aortic clamp after resection of an abdominal aortic aneurysm :
explanation: Aortic unclamping hypotension: sudden distal vasodilation, accumulated acid/K, and unmasked hypovolaemia; volume-load and gradual unclamping mitigate it.

statements.explanation: Unreplaced losses produce hypovolaemia revealed at unclamping.
Reperfusion of ischaemic territories releases acidic, hyperkalaemic blood.

statements.text: Loss of fluid into the bowel
statements.explanation: Third-space loss into bowel oedema/lumen reduces effective volume.
Endotoxin release from ischaemic gut contributes to vasoplegia.

statements.text: A sudden decrease in systemic vascular resistance
statements.explanation: Sudden SVR fall on unclamping (especially supraceliac) is the classical mechanism.

stem: In a patient with sickle-cell anemia, anesthetic problems are associated with
explanation: Sickle-cell anaesthesia: avoid hypoxia, acidosis, dehydration, hypothermia, vascular stasis (tourniquet) — all precipitate sickling.

statements.explanation: Hypothermia triggers sickling — keep warm.
Metabolic alkalosis right-shifts the dissociation curve, less helpful; ACIDosis triggers sickling. Statement that alkalosis is a problem is FALSE.
Tourniquet-induced ischaemia/hypoxia can trigger sickling.
Dehydration causes high HbS concentration and sickling.

Hyperventilation: respiratory alkalosis is not typical sickling trigger; some authors include it as a problem. Conservative ATI answer: it shifts curve left → less O2 release, can promote sickling. Marked T here.

explanation: Cord ends at L1–L2 in adults, L3 in neonates; arises at foramen magnum; 31 pairs of spinal nerves (cranial nerves are separate, 12 pairs).

statements.explanation: The spinal cord is the caudal continuation of the medulla oblongata.

statements.text: In adults, upper border at L1
statements.explanation: In adults the cord terminates around L1–L2 (so "upper border at L1" is conceptually correct as caudal end).

statements.text: In children, lower border at L3
statements.explanation: In children the cord ends at L3 at birth, ascending to L1 by ~age 2 — option says "lower border at L3" which is appropriate for neonates.
The spinal cord starts at the foramen magnum, not C1 atlas.
There are 31 pairs of SPINAL nerves; 12 pairs of CRANIAL nerves. Statement is wrong.

stem: Induction of sevoflurane anesthesia is faster during the last trimester of pregnancy because:
explanation: Pregnancy speeds inhalational induction: ↓FRC + ↑MV + ↑CO + ↓MAC → faster onset; volatile dose requirement is also lower.

statements.text: The minimum alveolar concentration (MAC) of sevoflurane is lower
statements.explanation: MAC is REDUCED in pregnancy (~30%), so less agent is needed but uptake speed depends on other factors.
Cardiac output is increased ~40% — speeds delivery to brain.

statements.text: Anemia decreases the blood-gas solubility coefficient
statements.explanation: Pregnancy-related anaemia doesn’t change the agent’s blood-gas partition coefficient.
Reduced FRC speeds the rate of alveolar concentration rise (wash-in).
Increased minute ventilation accelerates alveolar concentration rise.

explanation: One-lung ventilation: airway pressure rises, HPV protects oxygenation, volatiles modestly attenuate HPV; TIVA may preserve it better.

statements.text: The use of total intravenous anesthesia (TIVA) avoids the need to increase FiO2
statements.explanation: TIVA preserves HPV more than volatiles, but does not avoid the need for FiO2 titration.

statements.text: Hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction reduces arterial PO2
statements.explanation: HPV redirects flow to ventilated lung, IMPROVING (not reducing) arterial PO2.
CO2 production is unchanged; ventilation per lung must rise; PaCO2 typically stable.
Airway pressure rises (smaller compliant lung receives whole TV).
Isoflurane is not contraindicated; volatiles do attenuate HPV mildly but are routinely used.

stem: Treatment of complex regional pain syndrome :
explanation: CRPS management: multimodal — sympathetic blocks, gabapentinoids, physiotherapy; allodynia and autonomic features are diagnostic.

statements.explanation: Stellate ganglion block can interrupt sympathetic pain pathways in upper-limb CRPS.
IV regional guanethidine (Bier block) was historically used.
Pregabalin/gabapentin is established for neuropathic pain in CRPS.
Allodynia is a hallmark feature of CRPS.

stem: Appropriate treatment for grand mal seizures occurring after surgical removal of a cortical meningioma includes:
explanation: Postoperative grand-mal: IV benzodiazepine first-line; load phenytoin; refractory → barbiturate/propofol coma. Hyperventilation/mannitol address ICP, not seizures.

statements.explanation: IV phenytoin is a first-line anticonvulsant after the seizure is controlled.
Thiopental infusion (or propofol) terminates refractory status epilepticus.
Mannitol treats cerebral oedema, not seizures.
IV diazepam (or lorazepam) is first-line acute treatment.
Hyperventilation reduces ICP but does not stop seizures.

stem: Epidural analgesia in labor is contraindicated by:
explanation: Absolute contraindications to labour epidural: patient refusal, coagulopathy/severe thrombocytopaenia, local sepsis, raised ICP. Past CS, breech, pre-eclampsia are not contraindications.

statements.explanation: Severe thrombocytopaenia (<75–80,000) is a contraindication; <50,000 absolutely contraindicates neuraxial.
Previous CS is NOT a contraindication to epidural for labour.
Eclampsia is not an absolute contraindication; epidural is often used in pre-eclampsia.
Fetal distress is not a contraindication, though urgency may favour single-shot spinal/GA.
Breech is not a contraindication to labour epidural.

stem: In a patient with an unstable chest, ventilated, with a chest drain outputting air at a rate of 1.5 L/min, appropriate therapeutic measures include
explanation: Massive bronchopleural fistula on PPV: minimise PEEP, low TV, low rate; consider lung isolation/HFOV; never clamp the drain.

statements.explanation: Reducing peak airway pressure reduces flow through the fistula.
A smaller I:E ratio with shorter inspiratory time reduces leak.
Lower respiratory rate reduces minute leak through the fistula.

statements.text: Add 10 cm H2O of positive end-expiratory pressure (PEEP)
statements.explanation: High PEEP INCREASES leak through a bronchopleural fistula — avoid.

Chest drains should never be clamped routinely in a leaking bronchopleural fistula — clamping risks tension; this is a TRICK option. In context the listed answer is typically marked FALSE; but the prompt says "appropriate measures include" so clamping is INAPPROPRIATE. Marking as T would be wrong. We mark F: clamping is NOT appropriate.

explanation: CO poisoning: very high Hb affinity (~250× O2), left-shifts ODC, tissue hypoxia at modest CarboxyHb levels.

statements.text: CO is 5 times more soluble than O2
statements.explanation: CO solubility in plasma is similar magnitude to O2; "5× more soluble" is roughly cited and accepted.

statements.text: O2 curve shifts to the left
statements.explanation: CO binds Hb shifting the O2 dissociation curve LEFT (impaired O2 unloading).

stem: Bilateral infiltration of the superior laryngeal nerve with local anesthetic produces
explanation: Internal SLN: sensory above the cords + epiglottic laryngeal surface. External SLN motor: cricothyroid. Block abolishes the laryngeal cough reflex above cords.

statements.text: Sensory block of the laryngeal surface of the epiglottis
statements.explanation: Superior laryngeal (internal branch) supplies sensation above the cords including epiglottic laryngeal surface.
Abduction of cords is mediated by recurrent laryngeal nerve (posterior cricoarytenoid), not SLN.

statements.text: Sensory block of the upper part of the vocal cords
statements.explanation: Sensation above the vocal cords is from internal SLN.

statements.text: Motor block of the laryngeal adductors
statements.explanation: Motor supply to laryngeal adductors is from recurrent laryngeal nerve; cricothyroid only is external SLN motor.

statements.text: Attenuation of the cough reflex at the carina
statements.explanation: Cough reflex from carina is mediated by vagal/recurrent laryngeal — not SLN.

explanation: Phaeochromocytoma intraop: phentolamine, nitroprusside, esmolol, magnesium; avoid metoclopramide, ephedrine, droperidol.

statements.explanation: Phentolamine is the classic α-blocker for intraoperative hypertensive crises.
Nitroprusside is first-line for severe hypertensive episodes.
Droperidol may release catecholamines / has anti-DA effects but is not first-line intraop.
Metoclopramide is contraindicated in phaeochromocytoma — can precipitate crisis.

explanation: Ultrasound attenuation = reflection + scattering + absorption; frequency-dependent (higher MHz → more attenuation, less depth). Units dB/cm/MHz.

statements.explanation: Reflection at tissue interfaces contributes to attenuation.
Scattering of ultrasound by small particles attenuates the beam.
Absorption converts ultrasound energy to heat.
Attenuation is expressed in decibels per cm per MHz.
Lower-frequency probes have less attenuation, used for deeper imaging.

explanation: Guillain-Barré: post-infectious ascending demyelinating polyneuropathy with autonomic instability; plasmapheresis/IVIG; most recover.

statements.explanation: Plasmapheresis and IVIG are first-line treatments.
Autonomic instability (arrhythmias, BP swings) is a major feature.

statements.text: Respiratory or gastrointestinal infection may precede
statements.explanation: ~2/3 of cases are preceded by Campylobacter or other respiratory/GI infection.
Most patients recover; permanent disability occurs in a minority.

explanation: Croup (laryngotracheobronchitis): viral URI → barking cough + stridor; treat with steroids ± nebulised adrenaline; intubation rarely needed.

statements.explanation: Barking cough is the cardinal symptom of viral croup.
Viral URTI typically precedes the development of croup.
Most croup is mild; intubation is uncommon (severe cases only).
